fact: June 18 sits exactly 169 days into the year, which means there are only 196 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this date falls just 3 days before the summer solstice - the longest day of the year when the sun stays up the latest!
Fireflies typically start their blinking light shows around this time in North America, turning backyards into natural dance parties every night.
In Alaska, the sun barely sets on June 18 - some places get over 19 hours of daylight, like having summer all day and night!
Baby sea turtles are often hatching on beaches around the world during this time, using moonlight to find their way to the ocean for the first time.
The zodiac sign usually switches from Gemini to Cancer around this date.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, June 18 is deep winter - some places might even have snow while kids in North America are swimming!
Strawberry season typically peaks around this time in many northern regions, which is why the full moon in June is sometimes called the 'Strawberry Moon'.
Cicadas emerge from underground around this date in some years, making bug concerts louder than lawnmowers!
The sun rises at its earliest time of year around June 18 in many northern cities, even though the longest day comes a few days later.
Monarch butterflies are often in the middle of their epic migration north during this time, with some traveling over 2,000 miles.
In Scandinavia, this date falls during 'White Nights' season when the sky never gets completely dark - it stays a twilight blue all night long.
